source_name: Humbird et al. (2011) NREL/TP-5100-47764 — standard bioprocess uptime
notes: Annual uptime fraction; 90% is standard NREL nth-plant assumption (8000 h/yr)

source_name: Jouny Luc Jiao 2018 Ind Eng Chem Res — General TEA of CO2 electrolysis systems
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. Jouny et al. 2018 (Table 1) model formic acid electrolysis at 3.0 V cell potential and 90% Faradaic efficiency; the 0.164 kWh/mol value used here is consistent with their electrolyzer assumptions and falls within the 0.12-0.25 kWh/mol range in lab-scale CO2-to-formate literature. Replace with cathode-specific data when available. Earlier versions of this CSV cited Liu et al. 2017 PNAS for this row in error; that paper is about Xanthobacter biohybrid N2 fixation using H2 as electron carrier and does not address formate electrolysis energy.

source_name: Fan et al. 2020 Nat Commun — bismuth catalyst for formate electrolysis
notes: Fan et al. 2020 Nat Commun (paper title: Electrochemical CO2 reduction to high-concentration pure formic acid solutions in an all-solid-state reactor) report maximum formate FE of 97% on grain-boundary-enriched bismuth catalyst with formate partial current density up to 460 mA/cm2 (Fig. 3 in that paper). 0.90 used here is conservative for screening at industrial current densities; Sn/Bi cathodes literature range is 80-97% FE.

source_name: Complementary to formate FE — electrochemical balance
notes: H2 byproduct fraction = 1 - formate FE; used for mass and credit accounting. Fan et al. 2020 report <4% H2 + CO byproduct at peak formate FE; the 10% used here is conservative.

source_name: Stoichiometric — molecular weight of water
notes: Pure stoichiometry: cathode half-reaction CO2 + H2O + 2e- -> HCOO- + OH-. MW(H2O) = 18.015 g/mol. No experimental uncertainty. Jouny et al. 2018 walk through the same balance.

source_name: Stoichiometric — molecular weight ratio MW(CO2)/MW(HCOO-)
notes: Pure stoichiometry: 1 mol CO2 -> 1 mol HCOO-. MW(CO2)=44.01 / MW(HCOO-)=46.03 = 0.9561. No uncertainty.

source_name: Internal screening estimate — no direct experimental anchor
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: No published kg formate / kg NH3 yield exists for Xanthobacter autotrophicus or related diazotrophs grown on formate. Liu et al. 2017 PNAS is the seminal X. autotrophicus biohybrid paper but uses H2 (not formate) as electron carrier and does not report mass yields at scale. The 11.0 kg/kg value here is back-calculated from formate energy content (~256 kJ/mol) and N2 fixation thermodynamics (~24 ATP per N2 fixed including biomass synthesis overhead). Treat as a tunable biological parameter pending experimental validation. URL kept as the conceptual provenance for the X. autotrophicus pathway.

source_name: Internal screening estimate — no direct experimental anchor
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Biomass partition between SCP and NH3 in a formate-fed N2-fixing culture has not been measured at scale. 4 kg dry SCP per kg NH3 assumes most fixed carbon is routed to biomass while a fraction of N2ase output is excreted as NH3. Needs experimental validation. URL is the conceptual provenance for the system; the specific ratio is not in that paper.

source_name: Internal screening estimate — stoichiometric proxy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Biological urea biosynthesis requires 2 mol NH3 + 1 mol CO2 + 3 ATP + 1 NADPH per mol urea via the urea cycle. Formate demand for a urea-producing X. autotrophicus is back-calculated from the NH3 pathway scaled by urea cycle stoichiometry. No published value exists for a urea-overproducing strain.

source_name: Internal screening estimate — mass balance closure
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Lower SCP yield than the NH3 case because additional reducing equivalents are diverted to urea biosynthesis. Ratio is closed against the formate-to-urea estimate; not from any published measurement.

source_name: Matassa et al. (2016) Water Research — HOB biomass composition
notes: Matassa et al. 2016 (Autotrophic nitrogen assimilation and carbon capture for microbial protein production by a novel enrichment of hydrogen-oxidizing bacteria) report HOB protein content up to 71% which corresponds to ~11% N (multiplied by ~0.16 N-in-protein factor); 8% N used here is conservative for a harvest with lower protein content.

source_name: Empirical microbial biomass formula CH1.8O0.5N0.2
notes: Carbon mass fraction 47-52% is standard for bacterial biomass based on the empirical formula CH1.8O0.5N0.2 (Roels 1980 Energetics & Kinetics in Biotechnology). 0.50 is the widely used screening mid-value. Matassa et al. 2016 reports HOB composition consistent with this range.

source_name: Palakodeti et al. 2021 RSER — review of NH3 stripping from anaerobic digestate
notes: Palakodeti Azman Rossi Dewil Appels 2021 review of NH3 stripping from anaerobic digestate reports recovery 75-99% depending on pH (>9.5)
column9: temperature (>50C) and gas-liquid ratio. 92% is used here for an optimized vacuum/heated stripping column with H2SO4 acid trap. Earlier versions of this CSV cited PMC9219067 attributed to Soto-Herranz 2021 in error; that PMC ID is actually Wang et al. 2022 ACS Omega on EDI cation-exchange membrane separation

source_name: Internal estimate — single-effect evaporative crystallization screening value
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: 90% recovery is typical for single-effect evaporative crystallization at moderate feed concentration in the bioprocess literature. NREL Humbird 2011 uses similar values for crystallization steps in their nth-plant designs. No specific bio-urea recovery reference exists.

source_name: Palakodeti et al. 2021 RSER — review of NH3 stripping from anaerobic digestate
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. Palakodeti et al. 2021 review reports stripping energy 3-12 kWh/kg N (varies with temperature pH and heat integration); converting to per-kg-NH3 basis (NH3=17 g/mol N=14 g/mol so kg-N x 1.21 = kg-NH3) and using the optimistic end with heat recovery gives ~0.5 kWh/kg-NH3. Earlier versions cited PMC9219067 attributed to Soto-Herranz in error.

source_name: Internal estimate — caustic for pH adjustment plus acid trap
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: 1-3 mol NaOH per mol NH3 is the typical screening range for pH adjustment to >10.5 plus partial acid-trap regeneration. Palakodeti et al. 2021 RSER discuss alkali demand qualitatively. Replace with mass-balance simulation when the digestate composition is known.

source_name: Soto-Herranz et al. 2022 Membranes — gas-permeable membrane NH3 capture evaluation
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. Soto-Herranz et al. 2022 (Membranes 12:572 Evaluation of Different Capture Solutions for Ammonia Recovery in Suspended Gas Permeable Membrane Systems) report 0.5-3.5 kWh/kg-N at lab scale; mature commercial gas-permeable HF systems are reported below 1.2 kWh/kg-N. 0.30 kWh/kg-NH3 is optimistic for a heat-integrated industrial unit. Earlier versions cited PMC9219067 in error (that PMC is unrelated EDI membrane work).

source_name: Soto-Herranz et al. 2022 Membranes — gas-permeable membrane NH3 capture evaluation
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. PTFE/PP hollow-fiber NH3 flux 0.1-1.0 kg/m2/h reported in Soto-Herranz et al. 2022 across capture solutions. 0.50 kg/m2/h is a representative mid-range design value at pH >10 and 40-60C. Used for membrane area sizing.

source_name: Palakodeti et al. 2021 RSER — review of NH3 stripping from anaerobic digestate
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. Palakodeti et al. 2021 report 5-15 kWh/kg-N for heated air stripping; the 0.80 kWh/kg-NH3 used here assumes heat recovery and an efficient blower (lower end of the literature range).

source_name: Internal estimate — auxiliary electricity for evaporative crystallization
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: NREL Humbird et al. 2011 use auxiliary electricity in the 0.05-0.20 kWh/kg-product range for pumping and solids handling in evaporative crystallization. 0.12 kWh/kg is a screening mid-value; most thermal demand is in steam (see next row).

source_name: Internal estimate — single-effect concentration plus crystallization
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Single-effect evaporator requires ~1 kg LP steam per kg water evaporated. From a ~10% urea broth needing ~8 kg water removed per kg urea
column9: multi-effect or heat integration reduces to ~0.65 kg steam/kg urea. NREL Humbird 2011 use comparable steam factors for biorefinery dewatering trains.

source_name: Matassa et al. (2016) Water Research — HOB volumetric productivity
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE anchored to literature. Matassa et al. 2016 report 0.41 g CDW/L/h for HOB at continuous reactor steady state. The 0.42 kg/m3/h NH3 productivity used here assumes biomass productivity as a proxy and applies the SCP:NH3 ratio internally; needs experimental validation for a NH3-secreting strain.

source_name: Internal estimate — scaled from NH3 productivity
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Biological urea pathway is assumed slower than direct NH3 due to additional enzymatic steps (carbamoyl phosphate synthetase + urea cycle); 0.30 kg/m3/h is ~70% of the NH3 productivity as a conservative screening assumption.

source_name: Internal estimate — fermentation broth dilution proxy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Aerobic bioprocess broths are typically 2-15% product concentration. 10 kg water per kg NH3 implies ~9% effective concentration which is reasonable for a high-N broth. Consistent with NREL Humbird 2011 nth-plant broth conventions.

source_name: Internal estimate — broth dilution for higher-solubility product
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Urea is more soluble than ammonium salts so broth can be more concentrated. 8 kg water per kg urea implies ~11% concentration. Conservative screening assumption consistent with NREL bioprocess conventions.

source_name: Internal estimate — disc-stack centrifuge dewatering benchmark
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Centrifugation of bacterial biomass yields 15-25% dry-solids cake. 0.78 moisture (22% solids) is representative for a disc-stack centrifuge on bacterial cells. Matassa et al. 2016 discuss HOB biomass harvesting context; specific cake moisture is a process-engineering screening value.

source_name: Industry standard — dried SCP product specification
notes: Final dried SCP moisture 5-10% is the industry standard for spray-dried microbial protein products and is comparable to spray-dried fishmeal (8-10%). Matassa et al. 2016 reference this convention.

source_name: Internal estimate — spray and drum drying energy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Industrial drying 0.8-1.5 kWh/kg water removed depending on dryer type (spray dryer ~1.0 kWh/kg fresh water
column9: drum dryer ~1.5 kWh/kg). 1.15 kWh/kg is representative for a combined spray + drum train. NREL Humbird 2011 uses similar values for biomass drying.

source_name: Internal estimate — disc-stack centrifuge energy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Disc-stack centrifuges typically consume 1-3 kWh/m3 for bacterial biomass separations. 1.2 kWh/m3 is representative; NREL Humbird 2011 uses comparable factors.

source_name: Internal estimate — combined mixing and aeration energy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Aerobic fermentation aeration 0.5-1.5 kWh/m3/h depending on oxygen demand and reactor geometry. 0.85 kWh/m3/h is the mid-range for highly aerobic autotrophic culture. Consistent with NREL Humbird 2011 fermenter conventions.

source_name: Internal estimate — compressed air screening
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Compressed air to bioreactor at industrial scale ~0.1 kWh/kg air; N2 mass fraction in air is 0.755. 0.05 kWh/kg-N2 is a screening approximation. Replace with site-specific air compressor specifications for a given facility.

source_name: Internal estimate — stainless steel reactor base cost at 100 m3 (NREL framework)
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: 100 m3 stainless steel reactor purchase cost is in the 40000-80000 USD range based on NREL Humbird 2011 equipment cost methodology and current vendor quotes. 50000 USD is a mid-range screening value; replace with a specific vendor quote for project costing.

source_name: Humbird et al. (2011) NREL/TP-5100-47764 — reference reactor size
notes: Reference reactor size for Lang/six-tenths cost scaling. NREL bioprocess design reports use 100-1000 m3 fermenters as their basis.

source_name: Internal estimate — NH3 recovery train base cost at 1000 kg/day (NREL framework)
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Vacuum stripping column + condenser + acid trap at 1000 kg/day NH3 capacity. Estimated from NREL Humbird 2011 distillation/stripping equipment cost methodology. Highly uncertain at the screening level; replace with a vendor quote for project costing.

source_name: Internal estimate — urea recovery base cost at 1000 kg/day (NREL framework)
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Single-effect evaporator + crystallizer at 1000 kg/day urea. Slightly higher than NH3 recovery due to evaporation hardware. Replace with vendor quote.

source_name: Internal estimate — centrifuge plus dryer base cost at 1000 kg/day SCP
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Disc-stack centrifuge + spray dryer at 1000 kg/day dry SCP. Estimated from NREL Humbird 2011 solid-liquid separation equipment cost methodology. Replace with vendor quote.

source_name: Peters Timmerhaus West (2003) Plant Design and Economics — six-tenths rule
notes: Standard 0.6-0.8 scaling exponent for process equipment. 0.70 is the widely used bioprocess screening value. Peters Timmerhaus West 2003 is the textbook source; NREL Humbird 2011 uses equipment-specific exponents.

source_name: Wang et al. 2022 IJERPH — review of struvite crystallization kinetics
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE within published range. Wang Liu and co-workers 2022 (IJERPH 19:7204 Effects of Physicochemical Parameters on Struvite Crystallization Based on Kinetics) review process parameters for struvite precipitation; 0.05-0.15 kWh/kg-NH3 is consistent with stirred-tank reactor + belt filter press. 0.10 used here. Earlier versions of this CSV attributed PMC9222832 to Huang et al. 2022 in error; the actual lead author is J. Wang corresponding author B. Liu (Shandong Jianzhu University).

source_name: Struvite stoichiometry with Mg excess (Wang et al. 2022)
notes: Stoichiometric 1:1 Mg:NH4 ratio with 5% molar excess to drive >95% conversion at pH 8.5-9.0. Le Corre et al. 2009 (Crit Rev Env Sci Tech 39:433) and Wang et al. 2022 IJERPH confirm 1.0-1.3 Mg:N as the optimal range.

source_name: Struvite stoichiometry with P excess (Wang et al. 2022)
notes: Stoichiometric 1:1 PO4:NH4 ratio with 5% molar excess. H3PO4 is the typical phosphate source for industrial struvite production. Wang et al. 2022 IJERPH review concludes Mg:N:P near 1.05:1:1.05 optimum.

source_name: Wang et al. 2022 IJERPH — review of struvite crystallization kinetics
notes: N recovery as struvite is reported in the 80-95% range at pH 8.5-9.0 depending on competing ions (Ca2+ K+ etc.). 88% is the screening mid-value used here. Wang et al. 2022 IJERPH review struvite precipitation kinetics and recovery factors.

source_name: Stoichiometric — molecular weight ratio
notes: Pure stoichiometry: 1 mol struvite (MgNH4PO4·6H2O MW=245.4) per mol NH3 (MW=17.03) gives 14.41 kg struvite per kg NH3. No experimental uncertainty.

source_name: Internal estimate — MAP settler and dewatering at 1000 kg/day NH3
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Reaction tank + mixer + settler + belt filter press at 1000 kg/day NH3 capacity. Lower CapEx than stripping column. Estimated from NREL Humbird 2011 crystallizer/settler equipment cost methodology. Replace with vendor quote.

source_name: Internal estimate — MVR compressor electricity from sugar/evaporation industry
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Mechanical Vapor Recompression replaces steam with electricity via vapor compression. Sugar industry MVR evaporators report 0.3-0.6 kWh/kg water evaporated. 0.45 kWh/kg-urea accounts for crystallization step. Well-proven at scale in food/chemical industry; NREL Humbird 2011 references MVR conventions.

source_name: Internal estimate — minimal LP steam for crystal polishing in MVR
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: MVR eliminates ~90% of steam demand vs single-effect. Only the final crystallization polish requires LP steam. 0.05 kg/kg is consistent with industrial MVR practice.

source_name: Internal estimate — MVR + crystallizer at 1000 kg/day urea (NREL framework)
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Higher CapEx than single-effect evaporator due to compressor cost; recovered through steam savings at scale. Estimated from NREL Humbird 2011 industrial MVR equipment cost methodology. Highly uncertain.

source_name: Stoichiometric MAP fertilizer synthesis — NH3 + H3PO4 -> NH4H2PO4
notes: Monoammonium phosphate synthesis: 1:1 stoichiometry with 2% H3PO4 excess for complete neutralization. Standard industrial practice per Nutrien eKonomics technical bulletin.

source_name: Stoichiometric MAP fertilizer yield
notes: MW(NH4H2PO4)=115.03; MW(NH3)=17.03; theoretical ratio 6.755; multiplied by 0.97 conversion efficiency gives 6.55. Fixed by molecular weights and assumed conversion.

source_name: Internal estimate — neutralization + granulation at 1000 kg/day NH3
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Neutralization tank + spray dryer or prilling tower at 1000 kg/day NH3. Estimated from NREL Humbird 2011 reactor/dryer equipment cost methodology. Replace with vendor quote.

source_name: Internal estimate anchored to Matassa et al. 2016 H2 yield
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Matassa et al. 2016 report HOB biomass yield 0.29 g CDW/g COD-eq H2 (where COD of H2 = 8 g O2 / g H2) which is equivalent to ~2.3 g CDW/g H2 in mass terms. Combined with an SCP:NH3 ratio of 3.5 and additional H2 demand for nitrogenase (8 ATP per N2 fixed)
column9: total H2 demand per kg NH3 estimated at 3.2 kg. Treat as a tunable biological parameter; X. autotrophicus N2-fixing yields are not directly published.

source_name: Internal estimate — scaled from H2-to-NH3 by urea/NH3 product ratio
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Lower H2 demand per kg urea than per kg NH3 because urea (MW=60) packages 2 NH3 equivalents in a heavier molecule. Scaled from h2_to_ammonia by the formate-urea/formate-ammonia ratio.

source_name: Matassa et al. 2016 Water Research — Calvin cycle stoichiometry
notes: Calvin-Benson-Bassham cycle stoichiometry: ~0.44 g H2 + 1.83 g CO2 per g biomass for HOB (Matassa et al. 2016 Table 2 derivation). Stoichiometric relationship holds across CBB-using autotrophs including Xanthobacter.

source_name: Internal estimate — H2/CO2 vs formate yield comparison
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: ~88% of formate case (4.0) due to higher maintenance energy of autotrophic growth and gas-liquid mass transfer constraints. Xanthobacter autotrophic yields under N2 fixation are not directly published; Matassa et al. 2016 provide HOB benchmarks for non-N-fixing growth.

source_name: Internal estimate — scaled from H2/CO2 NH3 case
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Scaled from scp_to_ammonia_h2co2 by the formate urea/ammonia SCP ratio (2.3/4.0).

source_name: Badgett et al. 2024 NREL/TP-6A20-87625 — PEM electrolyzer system energy
notes: Badgett et al. 2024 NREL bottom-up PEM electrolyzer model: 50-60 kWh/kg H2 system level. 0.055 kWh/mol = 55 kWh/kg / 500 mol/kg. Stack efficiency ~70% at 2.0 V cell potential.

source_name: Stoichiometric — water electrolysis
notes: Pure stoichiometry: H2O -> H2 + 0.5 O2. MW(H2O) = 18.015 g/mol. Actual water consumption is slightly higher than stoichiometric due to cooling losses.

source_name: Internal estimate anchored to HOB literature productivity
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Gas-liquid mass transfer typically limits autotrophic productivity. ~70% of the formate case (0.42). Matassa et al. 2016 report 0.41 g CDW/L/h for HOB at moderate gas supply; literature ranges 0.2-0.5 kg/m3/h.

source_name: Internal estimate — autotrophic urea fermenter sizing
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: ~73% of H2/CO2 NH3 productivity (0.30) reflecting additional enzymatic steps in the urea cycle.

source_name: Internal estimate; Dijkhuizen Harder 1987 confirm Xanthobacter methanol metabolism
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Methylotrophic biomass yield Yx/s ~0.45 g/g methanol (typical for serine/RuMP pathway methylotrophs). With SCP:NH3 ratio of 3.8 kg/kg methanol demand calculated as 3.8/0.45 + ~1 kg overhead for nitrogenase energy = ~9.5 kg/kg. Dijkhuizen and Harder 1987 (PMID 2933486) confirm Xanthobacter H4-14 has the methanol-dehydrogenase NAD-formate-dehydrogenase pathway needed for methanol catabolism but do not report mass yields directly.

source_name: Internal estimate — scaled from methanol-to-NH3 case
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Scaled from methanol_to_ammonia by the formate-urea/formate-ammonia ratio (6.5/11.0). Methanol provides both carbon and reducing equivalents for urea synthesis. Strain reference is Dijkhuizen Harder 1987.

source_name: Internal estimate — methylotrophic SCP yield proxy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Methylotrophic growth is heterotrophic. Higher SCP yield than H2/CO2 case (3.5) but lower than formate case (4.0) due to methanol oxidation energy loss. Range 3-5 kg SCP/kg NH3 is reasonable for screening; not directly measured for Xanthobacter on methanol with N2 fixation.

source_name: Internal estimate — scaled from methanol NH3 case
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Scaled from scp_to_ammonia_methanol by the formate urea/ammonia SCP ratio.

source_name: Internal estimate — methylotrophic productivity proxy
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: Methanol is liquid so no gas-liquid mass transfer constraint. Slightly lower than formate (0.42) due to methanol toxicity requiring fed-batch control. Literature reports 0.3-0.5 kg/m3/h for methylotrophs (e.g. Methylobacterium species). Strain biology of Xanthobacter on methanol confirmed by Dijkhuizen Harder 1987.

source_name: Internal estimate — methylotrophic urea fermenter sizing
notes: INTERNAL ESTIMATE: ~70% of methanol NH3 productivity reflecting additional urea cycle enzyme steps.
